Entity_Description: Agassiz is the main townsite of the District of Kent in British Columbia's eastern Fraser Valley, on the north bank of the Fraser River about 98 km east of Vancouver. Roughly 5,000 people (2021 Census) live in the community, which is surrounded by dyked floodplain farmland known for dairy and corn. Agassiz has hosted Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Agassiz Research and Development Centre since 1886, and its annual Fall Fair and Corn Festival is a long-running agricultural tradition. Highways 7 and 9 meet here, with the Agassiz-Rosedale Bridge crossing the Fraser toward Chilliwack; housing mixes older farmhouses with newer subdivisions.

Long_Description:
Stand anywhere in Agassiz and Mount Cheam fills the southern sky across the Fraser River — a daily reminder that this community of about 5,000 people sits on a broad floodplain hemmed in by mountains. That geography is the first thing {COMPANY_NAME} thinks about when planning {SERVICE} here. The dyked farmland around the townsite drains slowly through the wet months, and since the November 2021 atmospheric-river flooding put Fraser Valley drainage on everyone's mind, Agassiz property owners have had good reason to ask hard questions about how water, ground, and buildings interact on their land.

Agassiz is the service centre of the District of Kent, and its layout tells its history. The compact townsite where Highway 7 meets Highway 9 is ringed by working dairy and corn operations — agriculture so central that the community celebrates it with the annual Agassiz Fall Fair and Corn Festival, and so established that Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada has run its Agassiz Research and Development Centre here since 1886. For a {BUSINESS_TYPE}, that translates into an unusually varied property mix: century-farm houses and outbuildings on large rural parcels, many of them on well and septic; modest in-town homes near the highway junction; and newer subdivisions built as Fraser Valley growth pushes east from Chilliwack. {SERVICE_LIST} looks different on each of them, and {COMPANY_NAME} scopes every {PROPERTY_TYPE} accordingly.

Coverage geometry works in Agassiz's favour. Harrison Hot Springs and its resort-driven properties are only 8 km up Highway 9, close enough that one dispatch serves both communities. Chilliwack and Sardis sit 16-17 km away across the Agassiz-Rosedale Bridge, so crews working the south side of the Fraser can reach Kent-side calls without long detours — which is how {COMPANY_NAME} keeps {RESPONSE_TIME} response realistic for a rural address that bigger-city providers treat as an afterthought.

The climate seals the argument for local knowledge. Eastern Fraser Valley winters bring heavier rain than Metro Vancouver plus occasional outflow freezes and snow, while summers run genuinely hot on the open floodplain. Materials, scheduling, and workmanship all have to account for that swing. {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP} — and it is why Agassiz residents keep calling. Request your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} today at {PHONE}.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 5,000 (2021 Census); main townsite of the District of Kent in the Fraser Valley Regional District
- Home to Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Agassiz Research and Development Centre, operating since 1886
- Hosts the annual Agassiz Fall Fair and Corn Festival, reflecting its corn and dairy farming base
- Sits on the dyked north-bank Fraser River floodplain, with Mount Cheam dominating the skyline across the river
- Located at the junction of Highway 7 and Highway 9; the Agassiz-Rosedale Bridge links the community to Chilliwack, 16 km away
- 8 km south of Harrison Hot Springs and about 98 km east of Vancouver
- Property stock ranges from century farmhouses on rural acreage (often on well and septic) to newer suburban subdivisions
